destination.vibe: Home turf of Tadej Pogačar — where 50 cobbled hairpins climb to 1,611 meters on a road built by Russian WWI prisoners, the Soča runs glacier-turquoise through raw limestone gorges, and summer temperatures in the low 20s mean you can push 80-mile days in the Julian Alps without a 5am alarm.

flights.outbound_dates: July–August flexible (Tue/Wed departures cheapest; book 4–6 weeks out)
flights.notes: Connects through Frankfurt (FRA→LJU, ~2h connection). DTW→FRA direct is available daily; FRA→LJU is operated by Lufthansa or partner Adria. Bike-as-checked-baggage: Lufthansa accepts bikes in a hard case or bike box for €100 each way (Economy) — add during booking or call +1-800-645-3880 to confirm and add before travel. Deflate tires, remove pedals, lower saddle. July fares from $807 off-peak; midweek July rates around $879.

flights.outbound_dates: July–August flexible (midweek best; avoid Friday/Sunday peak)
flights.notes: Connects through Vienna (VIE→LJU, ~1h — the shortest final leg of any routing). Austrian is the natural carrier for Ljubljana; Vienna hub has the tightest and most frequent LJU connections. Bike fee: Austrian charges €75–100 each way in a hard case; confirm at austrian.com or call ahead. Part of Lufthansa Group — miles earn on Star Alliance.

flights.outbound_dates: July–August flexible (August slightly cheaper than July peak)
flights.notes: Connects through Zurich (ZRH→LJU, ~1h30m). SWISS bike policy: bikes accepted as checked baggage in a hard case or properly boxed, €80–100 each way; pedals removed, bars turned, tires deflated. Book at swiss.com and add the bike as special baggage during checkout. Zurich is a large, well-organized hub with easy connections.

lodging.name: Reset Apartments — Bike Resort Kranjska Gora
lodging.highlights: Officially designated 'Bike Resort' in Slovenia's national cyclist-accommodation certification system — secure indoor bike storage, bike wash station, and drying room on-site. Apartments with kitchen from ~€65–75/night; walking distance to the Vršič Pass road start. The most logical base for Vršič days: you wake up, clip in, and the climb begins within 500 meters of the front door.

lodging.name: Hotel Kompas Kranjska Gora
lodging.highlights: Slovenia's premier cyclist-specialist mountain hotel — certified cycling accommodation with secure bike storage, bike assembly area, technical support, and route guidance. Listed by Cycling for Softies as the Julian Alps' top cycling base. Pool and wellness after big days. Walking distance to the Vršič climb start; 45-minute ride from Bled.

lodging.name: Hotel Cubo Ljubljana
lodging.neighborhood: Ljubljana Old Town — 400m from Triple Bridge
lodging.highlights: Cycle-friendly certified hotel in the heart of Ljubljana — confirmed secure bike storage, 400m from the Triple Bridge and Prešeren Square. Perfect first/last night lodging: collect your bike from the airport, stay one night in the city center, then head for the Julian Alps the next morning. Design-forward rooms in a converted industrial building; bar and restaurant on-site.

itinerary.overview: Ljubljana sits at the foot of the Julian Alps, 50km from roads that shaped Tadej Pogačar. The city itself is charming — a compact baroque-and-Art-Nouveau old town on a river bend, car-free center, castle on the hill — but it's not why you're here. You're here because 45 minutes north by bike the roads climb into the Triglav National Park, and within a day's ride you're at 1,611 meters on the Vršič Pass (the highest paved road in Slovenia, 50 hairpin turns, cobbled bends 5 through 9) and looking at the Soča Valley turquoise below. Slovenia's road infrastructure is well-maintained, traffic is light outside ski season, and drivers are cyclist-aware — a cycling culture rooted in alpine terrain and a WorldTour champion who grew up 40km from here. July temperatures in the Julian Alps average 20–25°C at valley level, cooler above 1,000m: no 5am alarms required. The logistical strategy for maximum riding: one night in Ljubljana at arrival, transfer to Kranjska Gora for the Vršič stage, then Bovec for the Mangart day, return via Soča Valley.

days.title: Ljubljana → Bled via Sava Valley — 75 miles / 120km, 1,200m climbing
days.morning: Collect bike at LJU, assemble at the hotel (Hotel Cubo has a designated space). Roll from the Dragon Bridge in Ljubljana's old town and head northwest through the Šiška district, following quiet roads and the dedicated cycle lane along the Sava River. The terrain opens into farmland and orchard country immediately outside the city — limestone ridges on your left, Karawanken Alps appearing ahead. Kranj (32km from Ljubljana) is worth a 20-minute stop: Slovenia's fourth city has a compact medieval peninsula above the Sava gorge and a good coffee bar on the main square. From Kranj continue northwest through Lesce toward the Bled junction — the road climbs gently and the Julian Alps begin to dominate the horizon in front of you. At Lesce junction turn south into the Radovna Valley: this is the quiet approach to Bled, almost no traffic, beech forest on both sides, the first views of Triglav National Park. Total Ljubljana → Bled: ~60km.
days.afternoon: Arrive Bled and claim your accommodation. Afternoon ride: the Lake Bled circuit is 6km of flat paved lakefront road (more touristy than scenic from a cycling perspective, but obligatory). The real climb is the short access road to Ojstrica viewpoint (600m ascent of 20%, unpaved last 100m — worth it for the best overhead Bled photograph you've ever seen, no drones required). Then the Pokljuka Plateau if legs are there: 18km from Bled with 800m gain, peaking at 1,330m. The plateau is the site of the Biathlon World Cup venue — wide open meadows, silence, no settlements, birch and spruce. The descent back to Bled is fast and clean. Total afternoon: 40–50km depending on Pokljuka extension.
days.evening: Dinner in Bled: Gostilna Pri Planincu (Grajska cesta 8) is the local choice in a town full of tourist restaurants — wooden interior, local prices, good Slovenian trout and strukljli (rolled pastry, savory or sweet). Then the walk up to Bled Castle (perched on a 130m cliff above the lake, lit at night) for the view of the island church and the Alps that you'll spend the next two days riding through. Transfer to Kranjska Gora the next morning (26km, easy flat road along the Sava) or stay in KG from night one.

days.title: Vršič Pass Queen Stage — 88km / 55 miles, 1,900m climbing
days.morning: Start from Kranjska Gora by 7am. The Vršič Pass road (Cesta čez Vršič) begins at the eastern edge of the village — you'll know it from the numbered hairpin markers starting immediately. The climb is 12km to the 1,611m summit. The first four bends are forested and relatively gentle. Bends 5 through 9 are the famous cobbled section — the original 1916 road surface laid by Russian prisoners of war, irregular paving stones, gradients touching 15–17% on the steepest ramps. Commit to a steady gear before the cobbles start; a gear change at 15% on rough stone is a mechanical. Above bend 9 the cobbles end and the surface is clean asphalt for the remaining 41 hairpins. Pause at bend #8 on the ascent: the Russian Orthodox Chapel (Ruska kapelica, 1916) is a wooden structure built in honor of the ~400 Russian soldiers killed by an avalanche here during construction — the most moving 5 minutes of the ride. Summit at 1,611m: the view north is into the Trenta Valley and Triglav National Park, the Julian Alps raw limestone all around, Triglav's north face visible to the east. 50 numbered hairpin turns total — the most of any pass in Europe.
days.afternoon: The descent into the Soča Valley: 26 hairpins on the south face (steeper than the north), the road carved into a limestone cliff above the Trenta Valley. At the valley floor, the Soča River source is 3km east of the road junction — a cold blue-green pool erupting from a grotto in the rock face, genuinely startling color. Turn west along the valley floor and follow the Soča to Bovec: 26km of flat road along one of Europe's great rivers. The water is glacier-fed and Tiffany-blue, the canyon walls 200–300m limestone, the road almost empty on weekday mornings. The Great Soča Gorge (Velika Korita Soče) just before Bovec is a narrow slot canyon where the river drops through layered limestone — the road runs directly above it. Total Kranjska Gora → Vršič → Bovec: 55km, 1,900m climbing. Allow 4–5 hours.
days.evening: Lunch in Bovec — there's a gostilna on the main square serving local Bovška trout (the river fish of the Soča) with polenta, and a decent espresso bar for a mid-afternoon sit. Bovec is a small alpine town (population 1,600) with an outsized outdoor-sports culture: kayaking, white-water rafting, paragliding. After riding, wade into the Soča: the water temperature is 8–12°C even in July — 10 minutes in the river is better ice therapy than anything you'll find at a hotel. Dinner: Gostilna Sovdat (the long-standing local choice in Bovec, traditional Primorska cuisine) or one of the terrace restaurants on the main square. Stay in Bovec for the Mangart day.

days.title: Mangart Saddle — Slovenia's Highest Drivable Road — 90km / 56 miles, 2,200m climbing
days.morning: Depart Bovec by 7am heading southeast along the Soča. At the Koritnica Valley junction (6km from Bovec), turn east and follow the Koritnica River upstream toward the Italian border. The Mangart approach road begins at the toll gate (~€10 for cars; cyclists are either waved through or pay a nominal fee — have small cash ready). The climb: 10km from the valley floor to the Mangart Saddle at 2,072m. Average gradient 8.8%; the steepest ramps hit 25% briefly in the upper section. Five rock tunnels cut through the limestone face — each is short (30–90 seconds) but completely dark. A front light is not optional here: it's a safety requirement for oncoming traffic and your own sightlines. Above the treeline the road becomes a narrow ledge on the face of the mountain, the exposure dramatic. Summit: Mangart Saddle at 2,072m — the highest drivable point in Slovenia and one of the highest in the Eastern Alps. The view extends north into Austria (Carinthia), south into Italy (Friuli), and east across the Julian Alps to Triglav. The Mangart peak itself (2,677m) is a scramble another 600m above; the saddle is the road's end.
days.afternoon: Descend back to Bovec (same road — early morning means you'll likely have it to yourself on the way up; afternoon may have more traffic). Continue south along the Soča Valley to Kobarid, 12km past Bovec. Kobarid is the site of the 1917 Battle of Caporetto — the Italian army's catastrophic defeat by German and Austrian forces, described in Hemingway's A Farewell to Arms (he served here with the Italian Red Cross ambulance corps). The Kobarid Museum (Gregorčičeva 10) won European Museum of the Year in 1993 and is one of the best WWI museums in Europe: scale models, diaries, photographs, the original battle plans. The outdoor walk covers preserved Italian defence lines in the hills above town. Allow 90 minutes. Lunch in Kobarid: Hiša Franko (one of the most acclaimed restaurants in Slovenia, Ana Roš's restaurant) is 4km north — if you planned ahead and have a reservation, this is the meal of the trip. Otherwise, the village restaurant on the main square serves honest local food.
days.evening: Return north along the Soča Valley toward Trenta and over Vršič back to Kranjska Gora (or arrange a transfer/taxi for the return leg if legs are spent after the Mangart day — the climb back over Vršič adds another 1,000m and shouldn't be underestimated at the end of a 2,200m-gain day). Transfer to Ljubljana for the flight connection: Kranjska Gora to LJU is 85km by road (1h15m by car or transfer shuttle). Most LJU departures are morning, so a final night in Kranjska Gora or Ljubljana makes sense before the outbound flight.

tips: Vršič cobbled hairpins (bends 5–9): the famous cobbled section hits 15–17% on uneven paving stones. Commit to your gear before the cobbles start — a gear shift at that gradient on rough stone can snap a chain or throw you off rhythm. Wide tires (28–32c) handle this surface more confidently than skinny race tires (23–25c), which work but require more attention. If you've already crested the cobbled section and need to recover, there's a flat pull-off just above bend #9.

Mangart tunnel lighting is mandatory: five tunnels on the climb, all dark, all cut through solid limestone. The longest is about 90 seconds at climbing pace. A front light is a safety requirement — for you to see and for any descending vehicles to see you. Carry one even if your bike normally doesn't run lights.

Alpine weather window: the Julian Alps generate their own afternoon weather. July mornings are typically clear; by 1–2pm, cumulus clouds build over Triglav and thunderstorms develop fast. Start Vršič and Mangart before 7:30am and aim to be off the exposed ridges by noon. The storms are brief but violent — lightning on an open limestone ridge at 2,000m is serious.

Tadej Pogačar's training roads: Pogačar grew up near Komenda, 30km east of Ljubljana. The Pokljuka Plateau climb (Day 1 afternoon extension), the Radovna Valley approach to Bled, and the Vršič Pass are all in his documented junior training routes. Slovenians are proud of this in a quiet way — mentioning it to locals in Kranjska Gora is the fastest way to get into a real conversation.

Soča River recovery: the river temperature is 8–12°C even in peak summer (it's glacier-fed). A 10-minute wade or submersion after Day 2 in Bovec is the best ice-bath substitute you'll find at altitude. The water is also safe to drink if you're filtering — the color is genuine (suspended limestone and glacial flour) not algae.

Bike logistics at LJU: Ljubljana Airport (LJU) is small and well-organized. The baggage hall has space to unpack and assemble bikes; most airlines deliver oversize baggage to a separate carousel or oversize belt near the main hall. Bring a torque wrench, spare tubes, and a multi-tool in your carry-on (remove from checked bag for flight) — Ljubljana city has bike shops but Kranjska Gora's are limited. If you need a repair, Sport Bernik in Kranjska Gora (Koroška cesta) is the main option in the mountain area.
